pages.nursingStudyGuides.addressUnderlyi: Adresse underlying cause, coach slow breathing, provide reassurance for anxiety-connexe hyperventilation, ajuster ventilator paramètres if mechanically ventilated.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.alwaysDocumentT: Always document the temps the critique valeur was received, the temps the fournisseur was notified, the fournisseur's response/orders, interventions implemented, and the patient's réponse to traitement.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.anxietypanicAtt: Anxiety/panic attacks, douleur, fièvre, early salicylate poisoning, mechanical overventilation, hypoxia-driven tachypnea.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.areTheMostAccur: are the most accurate indicateur of liquide statut changes. A gain of 1 kg (2.2 lbs) = approximately 1 liter of liquide retention.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.assessForSignsO: Assess for signs of avc (FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulté, Temps to call 911). A-fib is the most courant cause of cardioembolic avc.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.below735Acidosi: Below 7.35 = acidosis. Above 7.45 = alkalosis. Normal pH with anormal CO₂ and HCO₃ = fully compensated.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.calciumAndPhosp: Calcium and phosphorus have an inverse relationship — when one goes up, the autre goes down. Always vérifier les deux valeurs together.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causeWaterToShi: Cause water to shift from ECF into cells (ICF). Exemples: 0.45% Half-Normal Saline (½NS), 0.225% Quarter-Normal Saline. Use for: cellular déshydratation, hypernatremia. Caution: never give to patients with increased ICP (can worsen cerebral œdème).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeAl: Causes inclure alcoholism, malnutrition, diuretics, diarrhea. Signs: tremors, hyperreflexia, tetany, seizures, cardiaque arrhythmias (torsades de pointes). Infirmier: IV magnesium sulfate (with calcium gluconate at bedside as antidote), moniteur deep tendon reflexes, respiratoire évaluer, and urine sortie.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeDe: Causes inclure déshydratation, diabetes insipidus, excessive sodium intake. Signs: thirst, dry mucous membranes, restlessness, seizures. Infirmier: gradual rehydration with hypotonic liquides, moniteur for cerebral œdème, strict I&O surveillance.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeDi: Causes inclure diuretics (furosemide), vomiting, diarrhea, alkalosis. Signs: muscle weakness, leg cramps, diminished reflexes, cardiaque arrhythmias (flattened T waves, U waves, ST depression). Infirmier: oral or IV potassium replacement (never push IV potassium), moniteur ECG, assess digoxin toxicité risque.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeHy: Causes inclure hyperparathyroidism, malignancy, immobility, excessive vitamin D. Signs: "bones, stones, moans, groans" (bone douleur, kidney stones, confusion, GI symptômes). Infirmier: hydration with NS, loop diuretics, calcitonin, bisphosphonates.

Causes inclure hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, pancreatitis, rénal échec. Signs: Trousseau's signer (carpal spasm with BP cuff), Chvostek's signer (facial twitch), tetany, seizures, prolonged QT. Infirmier: IV calcium gluconate, seizure precautions, moniteur ECG.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeRe: Causes inclure refeeding syndrome, antacid use, alcoholism, DKA traitement. Signs: muscle weakness, respiratoire échec, confusion, rhabdomyolysis. Infirmier: oral or IV phosphorus replacement, moniteur respiratoire statut.

Causes inclure rénal échec, ACE inhibitors, potassium-sparing diuretics, burns, crush injuries. Signs: muscle weakness, paresthesias, cardiaque arrhythmias (peaked T waves, widened QRS, sine wave modèle). Infirmier: cardiaque surveillance, administrer calcium gluconate (cardioprotective), insuline + dextrose, kayexalate, préparer for dialysis if severe.

Causes inclure rénal échec, excessive magnesium intake (antacids, laxatives). Signs: decreased DTRs, hypotension, respiratoire depression, cardiaque arrest. Infirmier: arrêter magnesium, administrer calcium gluconate, préparer for dialysis.

Causes inclure rénal échec, tumeur lysis syndrome, hypoparathyroidism. Signs: often asymptomatic; may cause symptômes of hypocalcemia (calcium-phosphorus inverse relationship). Infirmier: phosphate binders with meals, dietary phosphorus restriction.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeSi: Causes inclure SIADH, cœur échec, excessive hypotonic IV liquides, and diuretic use. Signs: confusion, headache, nausea, seizures (severe). Infirmier: liquide restriction, moniteur I&O, assess neuro statut, administrer hypertonic saline cautiously if severe (risque of osmotic demyelination if corrected too rapidly).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.chaoticDisorgan: Chaotic, disorganized electrical activité — no efficace cardiaque sortie. Immediately defibrillate. CPR until defibrillator prêt. Epinephrine + amiodarone per ACLS protocol.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.completeAvDisso: Complet AV dissociation — P waves and QRS complexes are independent. Wide QRS with slow ventricular évaluer. Urgence: transcutaneous pacing, atropine, préparer for permanent pacemaker.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.comprehensiveNu: Complet infirmier Guides d'étude covering Électrolytes, acid-base troubles, ECG interpretation, liquide balance, and critique Valeurs de laboratoire. Aligned with NCLEX and REX-PN examen blueprints.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.confusionMuscle: Confusion, muscle twitching, hand tremors, hypoventilation (compensatory — shallow breathing).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.consistentEcgAn: Consistent ECG analyse requires a systematic approach. Use the same sequence every temps to avoid manquant critique findings. The recommandé approach: Évaluer → Rhythm → P waves → PR interval → QRS complexe → ST segment → T waves.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.consistentPrInt: Consistent PR intervals with sudden dropped QRS complexes. Plus serious — can progression to complet cœur block. May exiger pacing.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.copdRespiratory: COPD, respiratoire depression (opioids, sedation), pneumonia, voies aériennes obstruction, neuromuscular maladies (Guillain-Barré, myasthenia gravis).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.criticalOrPanic: Critique (or "panic") Valeurs de laboratoire are résultats that fall outside the Plage normale to a diplôme that poses an immediate threat to Sécurité du patient. When a critique valeur is identified, the laboratoire must notifier the infirmier(ère) or fournisseur immediately, and the infirmier(ère) must take appropriate action. Échec to act activé critique Valeurs de laboratoire is a courant cause of indésirable patient résultats.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.dkaLacticAcidos: DKA, lactic acidosis, rénal échec, diarrhea (loss of bicarbonate), toxic ingestions (methanol, ethylene glycol).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.dyspneaConfusio: Dyspnea, confusion, headache, drowsiness progressing to lethargy, tachycardia.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.electrolyteImba: Électrolyte imbalances are among the most commonly tested sujets activé infirmier licensing examens. Understanding normal ranges, clinique manifestations, and appropriate infirmier interventions is essentiel for les deux examen succès and sûr Pratique clinique. Électrolytes are minerals that carry an electrical charge and are essentiel for nerve conduction, muscle contraction, liquide balance, and acid-base réglementation.
Électrolyte imbalances rarely occur in isolation. Hypokalemia often accompanies hypomagnesemia, and correcting the underlying cause is as important as treating the Valeur de laboratoire.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.expandTheEcfWit: Développer the ECF without causing liquide shifts between compartments. Exemples: 0.9% Normal Saline (NS), Lactated Ringer's (LR), D5W (initially isotonic but becomes hypotonic). Use for: volume resuscitation, déshydratation, hémorragie.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.fluidAndSodiumR: Liquide and sodium restriction, administrer diuretics (furosemide — moniteur potassium), elevate HOB, daily weights, I&O surveillance, oxygène thérapie if dyspneic, assess poumon sounds frequently.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.heartFailureRen: Cœur échec, rénal échec, foie cirrhosis, excessive IV liquide administration, SIADH, excessive sodium intake.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.helpAssessTheKi: aide assess the kidneys' ability to concentrate urine. In déshydratation: urine is concentrated (élevé spécifique gravity > 1.030, élevé osmolality). In liquide overload or diabetes insipidus: urine is dilute (faible spécifique gravity < 1.005).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.hemorrhageVomit: Hémorragie, vomiting, diarrhea, excessive diuresis, burns, third-spacing, inadequate liquide intake.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.iWouldLikeToReq: "I would like to demander [spécifique orders: calcium gluconate, insulin/D50, kayexalate, répéter lab in X heures]."
pages.nursingStudyGuides.ifHcoIsAbnormal: If HCO₃ is anormal and explains the pH changer, the primaire trouble is metabolic. HCO₃ is a base — decreased HCO₃ causes acidosis.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.ifPacoIsAbnorma: If PaCO₂ is anormal and explains the pH changer, the primaire trouble is respiratoire. CO₂ is an acid — elevated CO₂ causes acidosis.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.ifTheOtherSyste: If the autre système (respiratoire or metabolic) is also anormal and moving in the direction to normalize pH, compensation is occurring.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.imCallingAboutA: "I'm calling about a critique lab résultat for [patient nom] in room [X]."
pages.nursingStudyGuides.improveVentilat: Améliorer ventilation (positionnement, bronchodilators, incentive spirometry), administrer supplemental oxygène cautiously in COPD patients, préparer for mechanical ventilation if severe, moniteur respiratoire évaluer and depth.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.irregularlyIrre: Irregularly irregular rhythm with no discernible P waves (fibrillatory référence). Risque of atrial thrombus formation → avc. Gestion: évaluer contrôle (diltiazem, metoprolol), rhythm contrôle (amiodarone, cardioversion), anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.ivFluidReplacem: IV liquide replacement (isotonic liquides for volume expansion), strict I&O surveillance, daily weights (same temps, same scale, same clothing), oral rehydration when tolerated, moniteur Signes vitaux for signs of improvement.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.knowThe77RuleFo: Know the "7/7 rule" for transfusion triggers — hemoglobin < 7 g/dL generally triggers RBC transfusion consideration. For platelets, < 10,000 = risque of spontaneous saignement; < 50,000 = saignement precautions needed.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.kussmaulRespira: Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid breathing — compensatory), confusion, fatigue, nausea, abdominal douleur.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.lightheadedness: Lightheadedness, numbness/tingling (circumoral and extremities), muscle spasms, palpitations.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.monaMorphineIfN: MONA (Morphine if needed, Oxygène if SpO₂ < 94%, Nitroglycerin, Aspirin 325 mg chewed). Activer cath lab for PCI within 90 minutes of arrival. Obtain serial 12-lead ECGs. Moniteur for dysrhythmias.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.normal275295Mos: (normal: 275–295 mOsm/kg) reflects the concentration of solutes in the blood. It is primarily determined by sodium, glucose, and BUN. An elevated osmolality indicates concentrated blood (déshydratation), while faible osmolality indicates dilute blood (liquide overload or SIADH).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.normalSinusRhyt: Normal sinus rhythm is the référence against which tous autre rhythms are compared. Criteria: évaluer 60–100 bpm, regular rhythm, one P wave before each QRS, PR interval 0.12–0.20 sec, narrow QRS (< 0.12 sec). NSR originates from the SA node and represents normal cardiaque conduction.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.nursingStudyGui: Infirmier Guides d'étude — Gratuit Préparation à l'examen Ressources
pages.nursingStudyGuides.overviewOfElect: Vue d'ensemble of Électrolytes in Infirmier
pages.nursingStudyGuides.phGt745WithHco: pH > 7.45 with HCO₃⁻ > 26 mEq/L. The corps has excess bicarbonate or lost acid.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.phGt745WithPaco: pH > 7.45 with PaCO₂ < 35 mmHg. The poumons are eliminating too much CO₂ (hyperventilation).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.phLt735WithHco: pH < 7.35 with HCO₃⁻ < 22 mEq/L. The corps has excess acid or lost bicarbonate.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.phLt735WithPaco: pH < 7.35 with PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg. The poumons are not eliminating enough CO₂.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.potassiumIsTheM: Potassium is the most commonly tested Électrolyte. Remember: IV potassium must be diluted and infused slowly (max 10 mEq/hr peripherally, max 20 mEq/hr centrally). Never push IV potassium — it causes cardiaque arrest.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.prIntervalGt020: PR interval > 0.20 sec, every P wave conducts. Usually benign — moniteur only.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.progressivePrPr: Progressive PR prolongation until a QRS is dropped. Often transient and asymptomatic. Moniteur; usually no traitement needed.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.prolongedVomiti: Prolonged vomiting/NG suction (loss of HCl), excessive antacid use, hypokalemia, diuretic use.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.pullWaterFromCe: Pull water from cells into the ECF. Exemples: 3% Saline, D10W, D5NS, D5LR. Use for: severe hyponatremia, cerebral œdème (3% saline). Caution: give slowly; risque of liquide volume overload and phlebitis.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.rateGt100BpmWit: Évaluer > 100 bpm with normal morphologie. Causes: fièvre, douleur, anxiety, déshydratation, hémorragie, cœur échec. Traitement: adresse the underlying cause — sinus tachycardia is a symptôme, not a primaire rhythm trouble.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.rateLt60BpmWith: Évaluer < 60 bpm with normal P-QRS-T morphologie. Courant in athletes, during sleep, and with beta-blocker use. Treat with atropine if symptomatic (hypotension, altered mental statut). Pacing may be needed for unresponsive symptomatic bradycardia.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.rememberHypoSwe: Remember "hypo = swells cells, hyper = shrinks cells." Never give hypotonic liquides to head blessure patients (brain cells swell → increased ICP). Use hypertonic saline cautiously — too-rapid correction of hyponatremia causes osmotic demyelination syndrome.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.replaceChloride: Remplacer chloride and potassium, administrer anti-emetics, discontinue offending médicaments, moniteur respiratoire statut for compensatory hypoventilation.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.sawtoothPattern: "Sawtooth" modèle of flutter waves, typically at 300 bpm with 2:1, 3:1, or 4:1 conduction. Ventricular évaluer is regular. Traitement similar to A-fib; cardioversion is often efficace.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.stelevationMyoc: ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) requires immediate recognition and intervention. Look for ST-segment elevation ≥ 1mm in two or plus contiguous leads. Territory localization: Inferior (II, III, aVF), Anterior (V1–V4), Lateral (I, aVL, V5–V6). Reciprocal ST depression in opposite leads supports the diagnostic.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.tachycardiaHypo: Tachycardia, hypotension, orthostatic hypotension, decreased urine sortie (< 30 mL/hr), concentrated urine (spécifique gravity > 1.030), poor skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, elevated BUN-to-creatinine ratio (> 20:1), poids loss (1 L = 1 kg = 2.2 lbs).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.theBodyMaintain: The corps maintains blood pH between 7.35 and 7.45 through three buffering systèmes: the bicarbonate buffer système, the respiratoire système (poumons), and the rénal système (kidneys). Disruptions to this balance résultat in four primaire acid-base troubles that nurses must recognize and gérer.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.theLungsCompens: The poumons compensate quickly (minutes to heures) by adjusting CO₂ elimination. The kidneys compensate slowly (heures to jours) by retaining or excreting bicarbonate.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.thePatientWasAd: "The patient was admitted for [diagnostic]. Relevant historique includes [médicaments, conditions]."
pages.nursingStudyGuides.thePotassiumCam: "The potassium came retour at 6.8 mEq/L. The patient's actuel Fréquence cardiaque is [X], and they are [symptomatic/asymptomatic]. The ECG shows [findings]."
pages.nursingStudyGuides.theRomeMnemonic: The "ROME" mnemonic: Respiratoire = Opposite (pH and CO₂ déplacer in opposite directions). Metabolic = Equal (pH and HCO₃ déplacer in the same direction).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.threeOrMoreCons: Three or plus consecutive PVCs at évaluer > 100 bpm. Wide QRS complexes. Pulseless V-tach: defibrillate immediately. V-tach with pouls: amiodarone, synchronized cardioversion if unstable.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.toPracticeStepb: to pratique étape-by-étape acid-base analyse with clinique interpretation.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.totalBodyWaterC: Total corps water comprises approximately 60% of corps poids in adults (less in elderly and obese patients). This water is distributed between two main compartments: intracellular liquide (ICF, about 2/3) and extracellular liquide (ECF, about 1/3). The ECF is further divided into intravascular (plasma) and interstitial (between cells) compartments.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.treatUnderlying: Treat underlying cause (insuline for DKA, liquides for lactic acidosis), administrer sodium bicarbonate if pH < 7.1, moniteur potassium (shifts with acidosis correction), assess respiratoire effort.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.vfibAndPulseles: V-fib and pulseless V-tach are shockable rhythms. Asystole and PEA are non-shockable. Know the ACLS algorithms for each.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.vomitingCausesM: Vomiting causes metabolic alkalosis (loss of stomach acid). Diarrhea causes metabolic acidosis (loss of bicarbonate from intestines). This distinction is commonly tested.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.waterFollowsSod: "Water follows sodium" — remember that sodium problèmes are often water problèmes. SIADH = dilutional hyponatremia (too much water). Diabetes insipidus = hypernatremia (too little water).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.waterMovesBetwe: Water moves between compartments by osmosis — from zones of lower solute concentration to higher solute concentration. Understanding this principe is essentiel for predicting liquide shifts with IV liquide administration.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.weightGainEdema: Poids gain, œdème (peripheral, periorbital, sacral in bedridden patients), distended neck veins (JVD), crackles/rales activé auscultation, dyspnea, S3 cœur sound, elevated CVP, dilutional hyponatremia, decreased hematocrit.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.wenckebachThink: "Wenck-E-bach" — think "longer, longer, longer, DROP, now you have a Wenckebach." For Type II: "some P's don't get through" — fixed PR intervals with randomly dropped QRS complexes.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.whenMonitoringI: When surveillance IV magnesium sulfate (e.g., for preeclampsia), assess three things: deep tendon reflexes (should be present), respiratoire évaluer (should be ≥12), and urine sortie (should be ≥30 mL/hr). Absent DTRs = magnesium toxicité.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.whenNotifyingTh: When notifying the fournisseur about a critique Valeur de laboratoire:
pages.nursingStudyGuides.whenYouReceiveA: When you recevoir a critique Valeur de laboratoire, you must: (1) vérifier the résultat, (2) assess the patient, (3) notifier the fournisseur, (4) implement interventions, and (5) document the communication and actions taken.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.wideBizarreQrsC: Wide, bizarre QRS complexes followed by compensatory pause. Occasional PVCs are courant and benign. Frequent PVCs (> 6/min), multifocal, R-activé-T phenomenon, or runs of PVCs exiger intervention (amiodarone, lidocaine, adresse Électrolytes).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.youDontNeedToBe: You don't need to be a cardiologist, but you must recognize lethal rhythms: V-fib (immediate defibrillation), V-tach (pulseless = defibrillation; with pouls = amiodarone + cardioversion), asystole (CPR + epinephrine), and PEA (CPR + treat cause).

pages.home.sampleQuestion.continueCta: Continuer vers la banque de questions
pages.home.sampleQuestion.sectionTitle: Un énoncé, quatre options — raisonnement pédagogique intégral
pages.home.sampleQuestion.sectionLead: This is the real format: exam-style prioritization, plausible distractors, then a rationale that states the correct action with pathophysiology and dismantles at least one trap option—same structure as the full bank, item after item.
pages.home.sampleQuestion.safeToTryLine: This sample is safe to try—read the full teach-through here with no account and no payment step.
pages.home.sampleQuestion.rationaleBody: Correct (A): Elevate the head of the bed. Orthopnea from decompensated HF worsens supine—increased venous return and pulmonary congestion raise work of breathing; raising the torso cuts preload and congestion before you add anything else. High-flow oxygen (B) is the classic trap: it treats hypoxemia, not positional overload. Unless the stem flags desaturation, oxygen after positioning follows assessment—boards expect the mechanism addressed first.
pages.home.sampleQuestion.stem: A client with heart failure reports increased shortness of breath when lying flat. Which action should the nurse take first?
pages.home.sampleQuestion.choiceA: Elevate the head of the bed
pages.home.sampleQuestion.choiceB: Start high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask
pages.home.sampleQuestion.choiceC: Administer a rapid IV fluid bolus
pages.home.sampleQuestion.choiceD: Place the client in a left lateral position
pages.home.sampleQuestion.examTip: NCLEX-style priority: match the intervention to the mechanism in the stem—orthopnea → reduce preload and orthopnea first; escalate oxygen when assessment shows need, not by default.

pages.home.objections.heading: Common questions
pages.home.objections.subheading: Les mêmes inquiétudes reviennent avant l’inscription ou la mise à niveau — ouvrez une ligne pour une réponse directe.
pages.home.objections.worthItAnswer: Free previews let you judge the interface and item quality. Paid access unlocks the full bank, deeper lessons, and exam modes for your pathway—compare value on the pricing page when you are ready.
pages.home.objections.countryExamQuestio: Is this for my country and exam?
pages.home.objections.countryExamAnswer: Yes—toggle United States or Canada in the header, then choose RN, PN, NP, or allied. Content, labels, and checkout stay scoped to that region and track.
pages.home.objections.rationalesAnswer: Every scored item includes a teaching rationale focused on safe nursing action and elimination logic—written for your exam scope, not a one-line key.
pages.home.objections.platformAnswer: Checkout runs on Stripe; access is server-checked for your tier. Progress saves to your account, and we monitor reliability so study time is not lost to mystery errors.
pages.home.objections.vsOthersQuestion: Why choose NurseNest over other prep tools?
pages.home.objections.vsOthersAnswer: NurseNest is a single pathway-scoped workspace: lessons, bank, practice exams, and readiness signals tied to your license—not a flat PDF bank or random video list. You see the same item quality in preview before you commit; paid tiers add volume, depth, and history—not a different product.

pages.home.platformPreview.title: Question, rationale, puis retour sur la préparation
pages.home.platformPreview.subtitle: La même boucle que dans vos sessions d’étude : répondre, comprendre pourquoi, ajuster selon les résultats — pas un PDF statique.
Énoncés et distracteurs calibrés sur le style de décision attendu par votre ordre pour le parcours choisi.
A postoperative client reports new shortness of breath, oxygen saturation of 88%, and increasing restlessness. What is the best immediate nursing action?
pages.home.platformPreview.optionA: Increase oxygen to 6 L/min and reassess in 20 minutes.
pages.home.platformPreview.optionB: Assess respiratory status now and notify the provider of acute decline.
pages.home.platformPreview.optionC: Administer the next PRN opioid dose to reduce anxiety-driven tachypnea.
pages.home.platformPreview.optionD: Delay reassessment until the next scheduled vital-sign check.
pages.home.platformPreview.cardRationale: Pourquoi chaque option tient ou tombe
Les bonnes et mauvaises options sont expliquées avec une logique sécurité patient — pour emporter le raisonnement vers l’item suivant.
pages.home.platformPreview.rationaleCorr: Correct : évaluer et faire remonter immédiatement une détresse respiratoire aiguë.
pages.home.platformPreview.rationaleWron: Recommandé : évaluer et faire remonter sans délai.
pages.home.platformPreview.rationaleExpl: Hypoxémie avec changement de comportement = détérioration. Évaluer et alerter avant les opioïdes, les contrôles tardifs ou l’oxygène passif seul.
pages.home.platformPreview.rationaleBull: Option A delays escalation while the client is unstable.
Option C risks respiratory suppression before the primary problem is addressed.
Option D postpones action despite acute symptoms.
pages.home.platformPreview.cardPerforman: Visualisez la préparation par domaine et la suite à travailler — le temps d’étude suit les données, pas l’intuition.
pages.home.platformPreview.adaptiveHint: Étape suivante : court bloc pharmacologie, puis un contrôle de préparation pour confirmer la progression.

pages.pricing.regionFaq.heading: Which country and exam is this for?
pages.pricing.regionFaq.subheading: Confirm region and exam track before you pick a plan so pricing, labels, and study content stay aligned with where you will test.
pages.pricing.regionFaq.usCanadaQuestion: Is this for the US or Canada?
pages.pricing.regionFaq.usCanadaAnswer: Both. NurseNest supports United States and Canadian nursing and allied exam preparation. Use the country control in the site header to choose United States or Canada. That setting drives region-specific pathways, labels, lessons, and checkout currency so US and Canadian preparation are kept separate.
pages.pricing.regionFaq.correctExamQuest: Will I see the correct exam for my registration?
pages.pricing.regionFaq.correctExamAnswe: Yes when your header country matches where you plan to test and you select the exam track that matches your registration (for example RN versus PN). The app scopes questions, lessons, and wording to that region and pathway. If anything looks mismatched, update country or track, then refresh the page.
pages.pricing.regionFaq.switchCountryAns: Yes. Change the header country any time to study with the other region's experience. After you subscribe, pricing and tax on renewal may still reflect your original checkout; use Account settings for billing details or contact support if you need help aligning subscription region with where you test.
pages.pricing.reliabilityFaq.heading: Can I count on NurseNest while I study?
pages.pricing.reliabilityFaq.subheading: How we run the platform—and what to do on the rare off day.
pages.pricing.reliabilityFaq.siteCrashAn: We operate NurseNest as production software: monitored infrastructure, error tracking, and rapid fixes when something breaks. No website is perfect, but hard crashes should be rare. If you see an error screen, refresh the page; when you are signed in, your progress is stored on our servers so you do not lose your study work.
pages.pricing.reliabilityFaq.slowExperie: Performance depends on your device and network. If a page feels sluggish, try a stronger connection, close unused tabs, and reload. We ship ongoing performance work so core study flows stay fast for typical connections.
pages.pricing.reliabilityFaq.studyReliab: Can I rely on this during studying?

Yes. Sessions, answers, and pathway progress are tied to your account on the server—not only in one browser—so you can sign in elsewhere and continue. If the site is briefly unavailable, wait a moment and return; your work should still be there. For access or billing problems, use Account and support—we treat stability as essential to exam prep.
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.heading: Afraid of wasting money—or that you are not "good enough"?
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.subheading: What skeptical students ask before they commit.
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.passGuaranteeQu: Will subscribing guarantee that I pass?
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.passGuaranteeAn: No honest prep company can promise a licensing exam outcome—regulators and your own performance on test day decide that. NurseNest gives you pathway-aligned practice, explanations, and readiness signals so you can see whether you are improving and where you are still weak. Use that feedback to steer your time; we are here to make prep measurable, not to sell a pass you cannot buy.
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.startingBehindQ: What if I am behind, anxious, or bad at tests?
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.startingBehindA: Most people feel that way at some point. Start with short sessions and let missed items feed your review queue—the product is built to suggest what to study next instead of leaving you to guess. Progress saves to your account, so shorter daily blocks beat burning out in one cram session.
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.tryBeforePayQue: How can I try NurseNest before I pay?
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.tryBeforePayAns: Use the free question preview on the site and any free tier or trial shown when you sign up so you can judge the interface, rationales, and pacing before you subscribe.
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.examRealismQues: Will practice feel like the real exam?
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.examRealismAnsw: Items target exam-style judgment and the scope of your track, including timed and adaptive-style modes where your pathway includes them. No bank can clone exam day exactly—you still face uncertainty—so pair realistic practice with your readiness and weak-area data instead of memorizing letter patterns alone.
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.refundRemorseQu: What if I pay and it is not right for me?
pages.pricing.learnerFaq.refundRemorseAn: Read the subscription and refund policy for timelines and eligibility. If NurseNest is not supporting your prep as expected, contact support within the window described there—we aim for fair resolutions. Stripe checkout also shows your total before you pay; manage renewal from the billing portal.

pages.pricing.faq.0.a: Plans are sold by pathway tier (for example RN, PN, NP, or Allied) and country. After purchase, that tier unlocks the matching question bank, lessons, and practice modes for that registration context.
pages.pricing.faq.1.a: Yes. NurseNest uses Stripe for checkout and subscription management. You can update payment methods and cancel from the billing portal.
pages.pricing.faq.2.a: Refunds follow the subscription and refund policy linked at checkout. If you have a billing issue, contact support with your account email.
pages.pricing.faq.3.a: Your bank stays scoped to the tier you bought. Switching tracks usually means a new plan that matches the new pathway.
pages.pricing.faq.3.q: Can I change exam tracks later?
pages.pricing.faq.4.a: Use timed sets when your accuracy is stable on topics, then full practice exams for pacing. The app highlights weak categories so your next block targets risk, not comfort.
pages.pricing.faq.4.q: How should I use practice exams?
